ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for green milk tea; mapped to parent class 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific green milk tea code exists. Mapped at category level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for green milk tea as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of green milk tea (approximately 500 g) is a typical serving size as sold at Singaporean beverage stalls and bubble tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Green Milk Tea — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 — exact match to green milk tea entry (cr_id D08010034). Extended nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are consistent with the HPB-anchored nutritional profile; input meta indicated a secondary cross-reference at 0.85 confidence for these fields — values retained as plausible and internally consistent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and other micronutrients not reported in the HPB record; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for green milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 31 estimated by analogy to sweetened milk tea and green tea-based beverages with low-to-moderate sugar content as reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, published in Diabetes Care — a peer-reviewed journal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (31) × available carbohydrate per serving (500 g × 10.1 g / 100 g = 50.5 g) / 100 = 15.655, rounded to 15.6. Confidence in GI estimate is moderate given the analogous basis; direct measurement for this specific formulation is not available.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar load (7.2 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 9 mg/100 g but non-dairy creamer may contribute saturated fat and palm oil.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened or reduced-sugar version of green milk tea, as the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.2 g added sugar per 100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g); liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid standard green milk tea due to its added sugar content; an unsweetened version made with plain green tea and low-fat dairy milk and no syrup is a suitable alternative per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer and trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), combined with added sugar (7.2 g/100 g), contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk when consumed regularly in large serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit green milk tea consumption due to saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and added sugar; substituting with unsweetened green tea or requesting low-fat dairy milk with no sugar reduces cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory added sugar (7.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat from palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer negate the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request unsweetened green tea with low-fat dairy milk instead of non-dairy creamer to preserve the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory sugar and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green milk tea is a popular freshly prepared beverage sold widely at Singaporean bubble tea shops, hawker stalls, and café chains. It is made by brewing green tea — typically using green tea powder, loose-leaf green tea, or tea bags — and combining it with milk or non-dairy creamer, along with sugar or flavoured syrup to taste. The drink may be served hot or cold, and customers can often customise sweetness level, ice level, and toppings such as pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. The flavour profile is mildly grassy and vegetal from the green tea, balanced by the creaminess of milk or creamer and the sweetness of added sugar. Per 100 g, green milk tea provides approximately 56 kcal, 0.24 g protein, 1.77 g fat (of which 1.0 g is saturated fat), and 10.1 g carbohydrate (of which 7.2 g is sugar), with very low dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (9 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at 31 (low), but the glycemic load per medium cup (500 g) is approximately 15.6, which is clinically meaningful for individuals managing blood glucose. The beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its added sugar content, making it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. An unsweetened version with plain low-fat dairy milk is a substantially healthier alternative. Green tea polyphenols (catechins) in the base tea confer antioxidant properties, but these benefits are partially offset by the added sugar and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer in the standard formulation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in green milk tea?
Can diabetics drink green milk tea?
Is green milk tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of green milk tea?
Is green tea with milk good for weight loss?
Green milk tea vs black milk tea calories
Can I drink green milk tea if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at 0.95 confidence for an exact match to green milk tea (cr_id D08010034). Extended nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are consistent with the HPB-anchored nutritional profile and retained at 0.85 confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2008) published in Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and moisture data for freshly prepared green milk tea are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Portions data from input contained multiple duplicate entries; deduplicated to three representative sizes (250 g standard cup, 500 g medium cup, 700 g large cup) for serving reference. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are retained as contextually accurate per 100 g but note that a large cup (700 g) delivers approximately 392 kcal and 63 mg sodium, which is not low-calorie in practice. GI of 31 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed literature; direct measurement for this specific formulation is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database.
